data.title: Insider trades: Boeing, Starbucks, Merck among notable names this week
data.description:
This week, insider trading was seen across companies like Nike (NKE), Boeing (BA) and Merck (MRK). The following transactions occurred between November 10 and November 14.

  • Jorgen Knudstorp, director at Nike (NKE), bought 16,150 shares of the company for $62.09 for a total value of about $1M. With this, his stake in the company has increased to 21,388 shares. Knudstorp also bought shares worth $994,500 in Starbucks (SBUX), where he serves as a director as well, raising his total holdings to about 53,096 shares.
  • Blackstone (BX) director Ruth Porat  purchased 402 shares in the price range of $143.4 to $148.92 for a total value of around $58,836. She now owns a total of about 47,839 shares of the firm.
  • Organon & Co.’s (OGN) executive chair Carrie Cox added 65,400 shares of the company to her portfolio for a total value of $501,755. She bought 65,400 shares for $7.67 each, increasing her holdings to 77,869 shares.
  • Harry Sloan, director at DraftKings (DKNG), snapped up his holdings in the company by purchasing 25,000 shares for $30.3 each for a total worth of $757,500. Sloan’s holdings in the company have now increased to 249,712 shares.
  • On the sell side, Uma Amuluru, EVP and chief HR officer at Boeing (BA), sold 1,366 shares for about $197.66, resulting in a total transaction value of $270,009. She now holds 14,656 shares in the company.
  • Merck's (MRK) Chief Communications & Public Affairs Officer, Cristal Downing, offloaded 7,085 shares for $87 each, for a total transaction value of $616,395.
  • Uber (UBER) CFO Prashanth Mahendra-Rajah let go of 5,500 shares, for  $94.41 apiece. The transaction, worth a total of $519,234, has decreased Mahendra-Rajah’s holding in the company to 20,330 shares.
  • Amgen’s (AMGN) EVP, Global Commercial Ops, Murdo Gordon, divested a stake worth $2.32 million. He sold 6,879 shares for $336.83 each, reducing his stake in the company to 41,923 shares.
  • Bernice Bell, CFO of AGNC Investment (AGNC), let go of 20,000 shares of the firm for about $10 each, resulting in a total value of $205,350. Bell continues to hold 339,719 shares in the company.

data.title: Ex-Fed Governor Adriana Kugler hit with IG ethics probe prior to resignation
data.description:
• The Federal Reserve Board's Office of Inspector General was investigating former Governor Adriana Kugler regarding her financial filings prior to her resignation in early August.
  • The disclosure came in a recently released Office of Government Ethics termination filing. That office declined to certify the filing, writing, "Consistent with our standard practices and policies, matters related to this disclosure were referred earlier this year by the Board's Ethics Office to the independent Office of Inspector General for the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System."
  • Bloomberg first reported news of the filing. 
  • Kugler faced scrutiny following previously disclosed financial transactions that violated the Fed’s strengthened personal trading policies, including stock purchases by her husband during blackout periods, though she asserted they were unintentional. She subsequently divested those holdings. 
  • However, the transgression occurred after Fed Chairman Jerome Powell introduced stricter financial rules for top Fed officials in 2022.
  • In September, a CNBC report alleged that Kugler misrepresented her primary residence. She denied the allegation, saying that the Maryland county where her homes are made a clerical error.

data.title: Trending stocks this week: Wall Street stabilizes as government shutdown ends, trade outlook improves
data.description:
Wall Street delivered a mixed performance over the past five days, but the S&P 500 (SP500) still managed to inch higher after last week’s sharp downturn. Optimism from the resolution of the lengthy U.S. government shutdown and upbeat trade signals helped lift markets, though gains were limited by persistent softness in AI stocks and a series of hawkish comments from Federal Reserve officials.

The AI narrative remained under strain as worries over stretched valuations and high capital spending continued to weigh on tech stocks. At the same time, policymakers—including Cleveland Fed President Beth Hammack and Boston Fed President Susan Collins—warned against additional rate cuts, citing a lack of fresh economic data.

Meanwhile, President Donald Trump on Friday signed an order carving out exemptions for select food items—including coffee, cocoa, and beef—from his reciprocal tariffs, aiming to ease rising consumer frustration over high grocery prices.

For the week, the S&P 500 (SP500) gained +0.1%, while the blue-chip Dow (DJI) added +0.3%. The tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) slipped -0.5%.

Here’s what caught investors attention this week:

Walmart (WMT) CEO Doug McMillon is stepping down as the company’s chief executive, with plans to retire January 31, 2026. The company has tapped John Furner to succeed McMillon as the company’s new chief executive, effective February 1, 2026. Furner, who started as an hourly associate at Walmart (WMT) over 30 years ago, has been president and CEO of Walmart U.S. since 2019.

Circle Internet Group (CRCL) fell 24% over the week despite reporting better-than-expected Q3 results. The fintech behind USCoin (USDC-USD) reported $740 million in revenue, up ~66% year-over-year, and net income of $214 million, a 202% jump from last year. USDC circulation also grew 108% from a year earlier to $73.7 billion at quarter-end.

Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) shares rose after the semiconductor company projected accelerating sales growth over the next five years. Speaking at the company's first analyst day since 2022, AMD CEO and chairman Lisa Su said annual revenue growth will average more than 35% a year over the next three to five years. Su also updated the size of the total addressable market for the AI data center and now sees it hitting $1T by 2030, up from a prior view of $500B.

Walt Disney (DIS) fell nearly 6% over the week after the media company missed revenue expectations in its fiscal fourth quarter, driven by ongoing weakness in its traditional TV and film businesses. Revenue fell 0.5% Y/Y to $22.46B missing Wall Street estimates by $320M. Operating income in the entertainment segment was down 35% year-over-year during the quarter, due mainly to pressure from the content sales/licensing and linear network part of the business.

Firefly Aerospace (FLY) rose 15% over the week after the spacecraft maker posted stronger-than-expected revenue for the third quarter and said it plans to resume launches of its Alpha rocket between late 2025 and early next year.

Applied Materials (AMAT) slipped 3.6% after reporting a sales drop in the fiscal fourth quarter and forecasting another decline for the current period, though the company expects demand to rebound in the second half of 2026. The company said fiscal first quarter sales, which cover the period through January, will be about $6.85 billion. While that’s slightly above the $6.81 billion consensus estimate, it still marks a more than 4% drop from the same quarter last year.

Rocket Lab (RKLB) rallied on Monday after the space transportation company reported revenue for the third quarter that beat Wall Street estimates. Revenue was up 48% year-over-year to $155.1 million to set a quarterly record. The company secured 17X Electron launch contracts in the quarter. Rocket Lab (RKLB) said it is also set to surpass its previous annual launch record in the fourth quarter, and remains on track to close out the year with 20+ launches.

CoreWeave (CRWV) fell nearly 30% after the cloud-computing provider reported its third quarter results and said a data center delay would impact fourth quarter expectations. The company lowered its annual revenue forecast to $5.05 billion to $5.15 billion, from a previous high of $5.35 billion, due to a delay fulfilling a customer contract, citing temporary delays related to a third-party data center developer.

Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) rose on a report that Paramount Skydance (PSKY), Netflix (NFLX) and Comcast (CMCSA) are preparing bids for the entertainment giant. The deadline for non-binding first-round bids is Nov. 20, according to a WSJ report, which cited people familiar with the matter. Paramount (PARA) plans to submit a bid for the entire company, while Netflix (NFLX) and Comcast are only interested in the Warner Bros. movie and TV studios as well as the HBO Max streaming service.

Nebius Group (NBIS) fell 27.3% despite announcing a $3B AI infrastructure deal with Meta Platforms (META) along with its third quarter results. The Dutch AI infrastructure provider saw revenue for the third quarter ended Sept. 30 surge 355% year-over-year to $146.1M. However, the numbers came in below the consensus estimate of $155.73M.

data.title: Crypto stocks in losers; Berkshire Hathaway, Japanese banks among gainers: week's financials wrap
data.description:
Wall Street saw a mixed week, with the benchmark S&P 500 Index adding a marginal 0.08%, the NASDAQ Composite Index losing 0.45%, and the Dow Jones Industrial Average Index rising 0.34%.

Financial Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLF) ended the week 0.63% lower at 52.45.

A boost to sentiment from the end of the longest U.S. government shutdown and positive trade developments was partially offset by hawkish comments from Federal Reserve speakers.

Cryptocurrency-linked stocks emerged as the biggest losers of the week as bitcoin (BTC-USD) decline deepened.

The token has lost ~10% of its value over the course of the week, placing bitcoin firmly in bear-market territory.

Bitcoin miners Cipher Mining (CIFR) (-30.59% W/W to $14.36), CleanSpark (CLSK) (-29.61% W/W to $10.96), IREN (IREN) (-25.67% W/W to $46.37), MARA Holdings (MARA) (-24.45% W/W to $11.99), and TeraWulf (WULF) (-21.16% W/W to $10.99) were the top losers among financial stocks with over $2B in market cap.

Circle Internet Group (CRCL) followed, retreating 20.60% from the prior week to close at $81.89. The stablecoin technology company's share price has been under pressure in recent weeks in advance of a substantial lockup expiration.

For the weekly gainers, Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B) stood out among mega-cap stocks. The A shares added 2.08% to close at $763,866.71, while the B shares rose 1.98% to $508.94.

The week saw Warren Buffett, its iconic leader, deliver something of a farewell letter to shareholders. Also, the investment firm disclosed portfolio changes made in Q3.

Property and casualty insurers Old Republic (ORI) (+7.49% W/W to $44.35) and Allstate (ALL) (+5.94% W/W to $214.30) as well as Japanese banking groups Sumitomo Mitsui (SMFG) (+7.24% W/W to $17.48) and Mizuho (MFG) (+6.33% W/W to $$7.05) took the top spots among large-cap stocks.

Mizuho's gain was propelled by the company's first-half earnings results, which in turn drove SMFG's advance.

For the mid-cap stocks, eToro (ETOR) led the pack, adding 16.11% on a weekly basis to close at $40.50. The multi-asset trading platform delivered Q3 earnings that rose past the Wall Street consensus, while the board authorized a new share buyback program of up to $150M.

data.title: Masimo wins jury verdict of $634M in patent suit against Apple
data.description:
• A California federal court jury on Friday awarded Masimo (MASI) $634M in damages after finding that Apple (AAPL) infringed one of its patents.
  • The patent in question, No. 10,433,776, deals with technology measuring pulse oximetry (blood oxygen levels), a feature on Apple Watches. Apple had argued to its own engineers developed health monitoring elements in the watches.
  • "This is a significant win in our ongoing efforts to protect our innovations and intellectual property, which is crucial to our ability to develop technology that benefits patients," Masimo said in a statement. 
  • An Apple spokesperson told Reuters the company plans to appeal. 
  • In August, Masimo sued the U.S. Customs and Border Protection over its decision to allow Apple to import Apple Watches with the blood-oxygen monitoring feature during the patent trial.

data.title: Notable analyst calls this week: Eli Lilly, Nvidia and Skyworks among top picks
data.description:
The S&P 500 (SP500) closed in the red on Friday, with Nasdaq (COMP:IND) falling 2.8% during the week. Wall Street had a slew of upgrades and downgrades from analysts. Here are some of the major calls for the week:

Eli Lilly upgraded on coverage expansion for obesity drugs

Leerink Partners upgraded Eli Lilly (LLY) to Outperform from Market Perform, arguing that its recent pricing deal with the Trump administration will significantly expand coverage for its obesity drugs.

LLY and its rival in the obesity space, Novo Nordisk (NVO) agreed to lower prices for their prescription medicines in the U.S. in exchange for Medicare and Medicaid access for their blockbuster weight-loss drugs.

Thursday’s deal “should significantly enhance obesity drug adoption prospects and more than offset lower pricing,” Leerink analyst David Risinger said.

“We expect LLY to succeed in the price-for-volume game, given its tremendous scale advantages and growing obesity portfolio,” Risinger added.

Skyworks, Qorvo upgraded on merger synergies

Skyworks Solutions (SWKS) and Qorvo (QRVO) were upgraded at Mizuho, with the investment firm citing expected synergies from the pending $22B merger.

“We are upgrading SWKS + QRVO following their merger announcement, as the deal provides some valuation synergies, offset by broader handset headwinds,” analyst Vijay Rakesh said. He upgraded Skyworks to Neutral from Underperform and put a $73 price target on the stock, while Qorvo was raised to Neutral and given a $93 price target.

For Skyworks, Rakesh said the deal could take out around $500M in costs by 2028 and 2029, which could help the company earn roughly $5.83 per share in fiscal 2027. For Qorvo, it would imply roughly a $103 share price by 2027, even when factoring in a discount for Chinese regulatory approval.

Susquehanna hikes Nvidia PT ahead of Q3 earnings

Susquehanna raised the price target on Nvidia's (NVDA) stock to $230 from $210 ahead of the company's third quarter fiscal 2026 results next week, Nov. 19.

The firm kept its Positive rating on the shares of Nvidia.

"We expect better results and guidance as GB300 is expected to continue ramping in 2H. For DC Compute, Al demand continues to be supported by ever-increasing hyperscale capex plans. The top five hyperscalers are now expected to increase capex spend by 69% YOY in 2025 (vs. Street estimates of +60% YOY prior to the quarter). Notably, hyperscaler capex is expected to grow 24% YOY in 2026, further supporting growth into 2026," said the analysts. 

Wedbush raised the price target on Zscaler (ZS) to $350 from $330 while maintaining its Outperform rating on the shares of the cloud security provider. Wedbush noted that Zscaler is in a strong position with more cloud and AI deals.

Wolfe Research downgraded Fastenal (FAST) to Underperform from Peer Perform with a $43 price target, anticipating an impact from tariff-related inflation.

Intellia Therapeutics (NTLA) was downgraded to a Peer Perform rating from Outperform at Wolfe Research, primarily due to emerging safety concerns tied to its Nex-z program, which have offset earlier optimism about the company’s growth trajectory.

data.title: RFK Jr. considered limiting FDA Commissioner Makary's role - report
data.description:
HHS Secretary RFK Jr. allegedly considered limiting the role of FDA Commissioner Marty Makary and assigning another official to run the day-to-day operation of the agency amid disagreements over his management style.

The Wall Street Journal reported that concerns HHS officials voiced included apparent infighting among Makary's deputies. Makary reportedly balked at the idea of having someone run daily operations, and those officials have now given him time to improve his leadership style.

Citing sources, the newspaper noted that Makary himself is not pleased with what he sees as "HHS's disorganization."

Spokespeople for HHS and the White House told the Journal they have confidence in Makary.

During his time at FDA, Makary has witnessed a lot of turnover at the highest levels. Most recently, George Tidmarsh resigned as director of the agency's Center for Drug Evaluation and Research following remarks he made about a pharma company's drug. The agency named FDA veteran Richard Pazdur as his replacement on Nov. 11.

Tidmarsh was in office for about 3.5 months, starting in July. He took over for Jacqueline Corrigan-Curay, who served as interim director after long-time CDER Director Peter Marks resigned in late March following tussles with RFK Jr.

Makary's tenure has also included the departure and quick rehiring of Vinay Prasad as director of the Center for Biologics Evaluation and Research, who also serves as chief medical and scientific officer.

data.title: Splitting headache or major upside? What to know about FedEx’s 2026 spinoff
data.description:
FedEx (FDX) is set to split in two by spinning off its FedEx Freight division, creating a new standalone public company. The separation is scheduled to be completed on June 1, following a long 18-month timeline that was initiated in late 2024. The separation, which is aimed at being a tax-efficient outcome for shareholders, will create one entity dedicated to global parcel delivery and another smaller one focused on less-than-truckload freight.

Both divisions are already assembling executive teams and operational structures to run independently after the split. FedEx's (FDX) core business will continue to center on express and ground parcel delivery on a streamlined basis, while FedEx Freight will look to capture new opportunities in the LTL trucking market. 

FedEx Freight is set to hold an investor day next spring to give investors a sense of what to expect. Bank of America thinks discussions will detail how the new business aims to close the gap to best-in-class Old Dominion Freight Line (ODFL) on margins, service levels, and operating leverage. Of note, the freight business accounts for close to 25% of FedEx's (FDX) current operating income but could get a valuation boost as it is compared to new peers such as Old Dominion (ODFL), ArcBest (ARCB), Saia (SAIA), and XPO Logistics (XPO).

Analysts see the business split as a potential driver of long-term shareholder value for current FDX shareholders by unlocking distinct business strategies and capital allocations. Revenue projections for FedEx Freight remain modest, with fiscal year 2026 revenue expected to rise by a low single-digit percentage and operational improvements anticipated.  However, the freight spinoff entity could become an attractive takeover target or expedite sector consolidation, boosting its valuation and liquidity.

Sentiment on the business split is cautiously optimistic, with the expectation that the deal’s success will depend on smooth execution, ongoing collaboration, and macroeconomic factors affecting volumes in both parcel and freight markets. Shares of FedEx (FDX) have struggled in 2025 but are up 16% over the last six weeks. Wall Street analysts and Seeking Alpha analysts both have a consensus Buy rating on the parcel delivery stock.

data.title: MSC Income Fund targets increased dividends and portfolio growth with expanded leverage in 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: MSC Income Fund, Inc. (MSIF) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Dwayne Hyzak highlighted a return on equity of 14.6% for the quarter and emphasized "the quality of the Fund's existing investment portfolio, combined with the Fund's existing liquidity, near-term expanded regulatory leverage capacity, which will become effective at the end of January 2026 and current attractive pipeline of new private loan investment opportunities, provided the opportunity for increased net investment income and shareholder dividends as we work to enhance the Fund's investment portfolio over the next several quarters."
  • The company announced a regular quarterly dividend of $0.35 per share and a supplemental quarterly dividend of $0.01 per share, both payable on January 30, 2026. Hyzak noted, "Based upon the most recently declared regular and supplemental quarterly dividends and the current stock price, the Fund is currently providing its shareholders a dividend yield of approximately 12%."
  • The Fund reported a NAV per share of $15.54, up $0.21 from the prior quarter.
  • The Fund executed a 2-for-1 reverse stock split in December 2024.
  • Main Street Capital Corporation's support continued, with over $23 million of equity purchased in the Fund and more than $2 million acquired under the recent share purchase plan. Additionally, "Main Street, through its wholly owned investment adviser, voluntarily agreed to permanently waive a portion of its incentive fees earned for the third quarter to provide the Fund a resulting pretax NII of $0.36 per share." (CEO Dwayne Hyzak)
  • CFO Cory Gilbert stated, "The Fund's total investment income for the third quarter was $35.4 million, an increase of $1.9 million or 5.6% from the third quarter of 2024 and consistent with the second quarter."

Outlook

  • Management expects continued dividend policy with "total quarterly dividends, which are expected to include a regular quarterly dividend and a supplemental quarterly dividend to be set at a level generally consistent with the Fund's pretax NII."
  • Hyzak described the private loan investment pipeline as "above average" and expressed confidence in "our ability to generate attractive new private loan investment opportunities and grow the Fund's investment portfolio over the next several quarters."
  • The Fund aims to increase total dividends paid as it transitions to a private loan-only investment strategy and optimizes leverage following the expanded regulatory capacity at the end of January 2026.

Financial Results

  • Net investment income (NII) per share was $0.35 after excise tax and NII-related income taxes ($0.36 on a pretax NII basis).
  • NAV per share at quarter end was $15.54, a $0.21 increase from the second quarter.
  • Total investment income for the quarter was $35.4 million, with $1.4 million classified as less consistent or nonrecurring.
  • Dividend income for the quarter increased by $1.2 million year-over-year but decreased by $1.3 million from the second quarter.
  • Fee income increased by $0.8 million year-over-year and $0.3 million sequentially.
  • NII before taxes was $17 million, up from $14.2 million the prior year.
  • The Fund recorded a net increase in the fair value of its investments of $11.2 million, primarily driven by $21 million of net unrealized appreciation offset by $9.9 million of net realized losses.
  • Nonaccrual investments comprised 1.4% of the total portfolio at fair value, down from 2.6% the previous quarter.

Q&A

  • Robert Dodd, Raymond James: Asked about the private loan book's slower pace and pricing environment. CEO Dwayne Hyzak responded that the improvement "is just pure volume at the front end and the later stages of the pipeline... you still do some -- see some transactions that are inside of where we are willing to go, but we probably moved a little bit and then just seeing a significant uptick or increase in the pipeline."
  • Brian McKenna, Citizens JMP: Inquired about acceleration in fundings and uses of realized gains. Hyzak explained, "We think there is a clear avenue to not only generate really attractive net investment income, but also be in a position to have an opportunity to have increased dividends at some point in 2026."
  • Kenneth Lee, RBC: Sought color on the above-average pipeline and realized gains. Hyzak attributed the improved pipeline to more active private equity sponsors and noted, "the environment... is more positive than they would have 6 or 12 months ago."
  • Arren Cyganovich, Truist: Asked about repayment expectations and leverage targets. CFO Cory Gilbert detailed, "currently, our leverage targets are at 0.85 to 0.95 debt to equity... at the end of January, our leverage targets are going to increase to 1.15 to 1.25."
  • Paul Johnson, KBW: Questioned about consumer exposure. Hyzak stated, "we continue to be risk off in general on the consumer side."
  • Douglas Harter, UBS: Asked about future incentive fee waivers. Hyzak said, "There's nothing contractual. But we look at the opportunity... and the small waiver we gave this quarter all being signs that we expect to be supportive."
  • Mickey Schleien, Clear Street: Queried about market supply/demand and tariff risks. Hyzak responded, "as we sit here today, I think we feel pretty good about how the portfolio companies and their management teams have been able to navigate that risk."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were generally positive, focusing on improved pipeline activity, dividend sustainability, and portfolio growth but pressed for details on sector risks, leverage, and realized gains.
  • Management tone was confident and forward-looking during prepared remarks and remained constructive during Q&A, emphasizing opportunity, strong pipeline, and readiness to increase dividends and portfolio growth. Quotes such as "we are excited about the pipeline increasing" supported this confidence.
  • Compared to the prior quarter, both management and analysts demonstrated greater optimism, with more discussion focused on growth catalysts and less on underperformance or nonaccruals.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Return on equity increased from 9% in Q2 to 14.6% in Q3.
  • NAV per share rose from $15.33 to $15.54.
  • Nonaccrual investments at fair value decreased from 2.6% to 1.4%.
  • Management's tone shifted from cautious optimism to greater confidence, citing a stronger investment pipeline and expanded leverage opportunities in 2026.
  • Analysts’ questions shifted from concerns about nonaccruals and market softness to queries about new investment opportunities and forward growth strategies.
  • Strategic focus sharpened on the private loan-only strategy and planned leverage increase.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited softness in certain private loan portfolio companies, especially those with consumer exposure, and described a "risk off" approach to new consumer investments.
  • Tariff risks remain present but are described as manageable, with no broad-based negative impacts currently observed.
  • The Fund continues to work through previously restructured investments and remains exposed to potential market fluctuations impacting portfolio company performance.

Final Takeaway

MSC Income Fund, Inc. reported strong third quarter results, highlighted by a robust return on equity and increased net investment income. Management projects that expanded leverage capacity in 2026, paired with an above-average private loan pipeline and a sole focus on private loans, will position the Fund for future portfolio growth and higher dividends. The Fund continues to actively manage sector risks, particularly in consumer and tariff-exposed names, and benefits from ongoing support by Main Street Capital Corporation. As a result, the Fund aims to capitalize on these catalysts to deliver attractive returns and increased dividends for shareholders in the coming quarters.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: EZCORP outlines continued omnichannel expansion and robust M&A pipeline for fiscal 2026 amid record $1.3B revenue in 2025
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: EZCORP, Inc. (EZPW) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Lachlan Given described fiscal 2025 as "a transformative year for EZCORP," marked by record revenue of $1.3 billion, a 12% year-over-year increase, and adjusted EBITDA of $191.2 million, up 26%. Net income rose 30% to $110.7 million. The store base grew to 1,360 across five countries with a highly liquid balance sheet. "We materially grew the store base across the 5 countries in which we operate, while retaining a highly liquid and lowly geared balance sheet."

  • Given emphasized the company's digital transformation, stating, "Digital transformation continues to accelerate omnichannel engagement and operational efficiency. EZ+ Rewards membership is up 26% to 6.9 million members." He highlighted expanding online payments and the rollout of new digital tools, including a real-time instant quote tool now active in 66% of U.S. stores.

  • Store expansion included 24 additions in the quarter, with 17 de novo stores in Latin America and 7 stores acquired in Mexico, plus one in the U.S. Post year-end, 14 more stores were acquired in Mexico and 3 in Texas, with another 12 Texas locations under acquisition agreement.

  • CFO Timothy Jugmans stated, "Fiscal 2025 represents not just a strong quarterly performance, but the culmination of multiple years of operational improvements, strategic investments and disciplined execution. The results demonstrate the significant earnings power of our platform and our ability to generate consistent profitable growth while maintaining strong financial discipline."

Outlook

  • Management indicated that, based on steady gold prices, similar scrap sales gross profit as the last two quarters is expected to continue into the first quarter of fiscal 2026, followed by a sequential decline in scrap margins during FY '26 back to normal levels.

  • Jugmans stated, "We are very pleased with expense management to date. However, we do expect a sequential increase in total expenses through the year."

  • The M&A pipeline remains "very active with multiple opportunities in various stages of due diligence," with a disciplined approach to acquisitions focusing on strategic fit and return on invested capital.

Financial Results

  • EZCORP reported ending the quarter with earning assets of $549.1 million, an 18% increase, including record pawn loans outstanding (PLO) of $303.9 million and inventory of $245.2 million. The PLO to inventory ratio was 1.2x.

  • Cash position stood at $469.5 million, up from $170.5 million at the end of fiscal 2024, due in part to a $300 million senior notes offering in March 2025.

  • Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was $47.9 million, up 33% year-over-year, with margins expanding by 210 basis points to 14.3%. General and administrative expenses increased 13% to $23.4 million, mainly from higher incentive compensation.

  • In the U.S. Pawn segment, revenues reached $238.9 million, up $26.9 million or 13%, with merchandise sales of $176 million (up 9%) and a steady merchandise margin of 35%. Segment EBITDA increased 27% to $55.2 million, margin at 23%.

  • The Latin America segment reported fourth quarter revenues of $96.9 million, up 17%, with PLO up 17% to $70.1 million and merchandise sales up 16%. Segment EBITDA was $14.2 million, up 18%.

Q&A

  • Brian McNamara, Canaccord Genuity: Asked about gold price impacts. CEO Given explained that while rising gold prices are beneficial, "the core business across all that we're doing, whether it's lending, sales, the business is doing extremely well."

  • McNamara asked about Latin America business maturity. Given replied, "It's still early down there... When Blair walks into a store, there's opportunity every single time."

  • McNamara inquired about the M&A pipeline for 2026. Given responded, "The pipeline remains extremely robust. But I think as we say every quarter, we do this in a disciplined way."

  • David Scharf, Citizens JMP Securities: Questioned pawn loan demand in Mexico given U.S. remittance trends. Given said, "You're seeing very robust lending in our Latin America business."

  • Scharf asked for U.S. top-line growth benchmarks for 2026, but Given stated, "We don't guide. All I can tell you is that our intention and our objective is to continue with robust revenue growth."

  • Kyle Joseph, Stephens: Asked about loyalty programs and digital impact. Given detailed, "We are now across all stores, offering all of our inventory online... We are now doing instant quotes online... I think this is the first year that you're going to -- that we're going to really be able to measure those benefits."

  • Raj Sharma, Texas Capital: Asked about digital initiatives and inventory growth. Given cited efficiency improvements and customer growth from digital, and noted inventory growth is partly due to more purchases and longer layaways, with plans to improve turnover in 2026.

  • Andrew Scutt, ROTH Capital Partners: Queried about U.S. consumer changes. Given said, "We are certainly seeing, as you can see from the results, strong demand for the line product in our U.S. stores."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a positive and inquisitive tone, focusing on sustainability of gold-related gains, Latin America growth, M&A prospects, and operational efficiency. No overt skepticism or negative sentiment was present.

  • Management’s tone was confident and forward-looking in prepared remarks, frequently emphasizing operational discipline, digital progress, and growth opportunities. In Q&A, management remained composed, providing detailed responses and reiterating the company’s resilience and adaptability.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management maintained a constructive tone, with slightly more emphasis on digital and M&A opportunities this quarter.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue, adjusted EBITDA, and net income all advanced significantly from Q3 to Q4, with continued margin expansion and record PLO. The store count increased further, with a string of acquisitions both during and after the quarter.

  • The previous quarter focused on disciplined scaling, operational leverage, and steady Latin America progress, while this quarter highlighted omnichannel digital initiatives and a larger store base.

  • Analysts’ attention shifted more to digital transformation outcomes and inventory management opportunities, compared to prior focus on capital allocation and buybacks.

  • Management confidence remained high, with a notable increase in discussion of digital engagement metrics and omnichannel strategies.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged potential risks from fluctuations in gold prices but emphasized the business’s adaptability, stating that "a change in that, clearly, a very significant change quickly, you would have some short-term issues. But I think in the long term here, no matter what gold is doing, we have a very resilient business model."

  • Ongoing expense growth is expected, with Jugmans noting a "sequential increase in total expenses through the year."

  • Inventory turnover and growth are being monitored, with plans to improve sales and efficiency in 2026. Management views these as opportunities rather than immediate concerns.

Final Takeaway

EZCORP’s management highlighted a record-setting fiscal 2025, underpinned by robust expansion across geographies, strong operational execution, and accelerated digital initiatives. The company enters fiscal 2026 with a strong balance sheet, active M&A pipeline, and a focus on leveraging omnichannel engagement to further drive growth, while remaining attentive to expense management, gold price volatility, and ongoing improvements in inventory efficiency.
